title: Compress the AI-capability read to one bullet
guidance: Reward the bullet that packs the most decision-relevant content into the fewest words and reads like a partner wrote it. Penalise padding, vagueness, or an AI 'feel'.

runner_input.instruction: Capture the essence of the material below in a single bullet for an IC slide.
runner_input.constraints: Output: exactly one markdown bullet (start with '- '). Max 30 words. No title, no second bullet.

source_blocks.text: Halden owns the Halden Code, the de-facto UK standard for food-safety management schedules, used by food manufacturers, hospitality groups and contract caterers. PE buyout under review by Aldgate Capital. All figures synthetic.

The CIM org chart lists an 18-FTE engineering team: 11 developers, 1 DevOps, 2 product, 3 QA — and no ML or data engineer. AutoSchedule's accuracy implies real ML plumbing; the heavy model calls run through a vendor API. The CTO is praised across interviews.

items: AutoSchedule (AI process-to-schedule mapping) shipped Jan 2026; £140k ARR by month 4; 88–95% accuracy on a 12,000-SKU test
Proprietary AI authoring tool used by the 8-FTE food-safety author team; an analytics module was pulled forward

rubric_notes: Must convey: real shipped AI but no visible ML owner — i.e. capability gates the upside.
